# Claims — Range 4
**Source:** Clive Gamble, *The Palaeolithic Societies of Europe* (Cambridge UP, 1999)  
**Wiki slug:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Slice:** `range_4_8936_13705.txt` (full-book lines 8936–13705)  
**Claim ID prefix:** R4-  
**Coverage note:** Slice opens mid-Chapter 5 (tables/absolute dates garbled in OCR); readable prose from ~p.184 through Ch.5 Summary, then start of Ch.6. Prioritize load-bearing social/network, transfer-distance, site, and Mousterian-variability claims.

---

### Claim R4-001
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble (1999) treats absolute dates for the 300–60 Kyr BP Neanderthal period as **age estimates**, not C14-style precise dates; caution is required when discussing Neanderthal habitat preferences.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `gamble-clive`, `neanderthals`, `radiocarbon-dating`, `thermoluminescence-dating`  
**Grounding:** “So, while absolute dates for this period are very welcome, we should not conclude that all those listed in Table 5.2 are unproblematic. Neither should we regard these as precise dates, as we might with C14, but rather as age estimates. This caution will be important when it comes to discussing the habitat preferences of Neanderthals.”

### Claim R4-002
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble prefers a broad chronological frame spanning the **Saalian (OIS 8–6)** and more than half of the last interglacial/glacial cycle (**OIS 5e/5d–4**), including three cool/glacial phases (8, 6, 5d–4) and two interglacials (7, 5e).  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`, `neanderthals`  
**Grounding:** “Our period spans the Saalian (OIS 8-6) and more than half of the last interglacial/glacial cycle (OIS 5e/5d-4). This includes three cool/glacial phases (OIS 8, 6 and 5d-4) and two interglacials (OIS 7 and 5e).”

### Claim R4-003
**Register:** attributed (secondary synthesis)  
**Claim:** Gamble reports that from OIS 8 onward the **mammoth steppe** (Guthrie 1990) and *Mammuthus*/*Coelodonta* faunal complex dominate northern biota, extending into much of northern Asia.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `neanderthals`  
**Grounding:** “It is from this stage that the mammoth steppe (Guthrie 1990) and the Mammuthus/Coelodonta faunal complex (Khalke 1994) dominate vegetation and animal communities… this distinctive biotic province extends to take in much of northern Asia.”

### Claim R4-004
**Register:** attributed (secondary)  
**Claim:** Gamble cites Jouzel et al. (1993) that OIS 6 cold lasted ~**200–140 Kyr BP** — longer than LGM cold — though Greenland ice suggests OIS 6 was less cold/variable than the last glaciation; van Andel & Tzedakis reconstruct extensive cold steppes in southern/Mediterranean Europe and a non-analogue tundra–steppe corridor between Alpine and Scandinavian ice.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Jouzel et al. (1993:411) use this core to show that the OIS 6 cold stage lasted from 200-140 Kyr BP… Van Andel and Tzedakis… reconstruction of conditions at the height of glaciation in OIS 6… Extensive cold steppes… No modern analogue exists.”

### Claim R4-005
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble argues interglacial climates (especially OIS 5e via GRIP) fluctuated with **rapid internal temperature shifts within individual lifetimes**, making millennia of constant climate/sea level/plant cover an unlikely scenario for 300–60 Kyr BP.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-clive`  
**Grounding:** “What all this tells us is that interglacial climates fluctuated with rapid internal shifts of temperature. These changes happened within the lifetime of individuals. Hominid adaptation to several millennia of constant climate, sea level and presumably plant cover is looking less and less likely as a scenario for the period 300-60 Kyr BP.”

### Claim R4-006
**Register:** attributed (finding via Mania / others)  
**Claim:** At **Neumark-Nord** (Geisel valley, eastern Germany), Mania dates two lake shorelines (levels 4, 6) to OIS 7 while others prefer OIS 5e; shorelines yield butchered bone and lithics (upper shore 654 tools; lower 250), while mud levels preserve drowned fallow deer without artifacts.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Notes:** **NEW SITE** — `neumark-nord` (not in allowed target list).  
**Grounding:** “Mania… argues for an OIS 7 date… although… many others prefer a 5e age… In levels 5 and 8… twenty-five partial or complete fallow deer skeletons… not associated with artifacts… By contrast, in the shoreline deposits, levels 4 and 6… Artifacts… (Table 5.4).”

### Claim R4-007
**Register:** attributed (interpretive caution)  
**Claim:** Gamble judges the Neumark-Nord lower-shore **aurochs–lithic association plausible but open to doubt**: similar low lithic density occurs lakeside without carcasses; gathering may relate to trees/anvil affordances rather than butchery. No cut marks on the aurochs; wolf-like gnawing present.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `gamble-clive`  
**Grounding:** “The case for a direct association between the bones and the stones is very plausible, but still open to some doubt since elsewhere at Neumark-Nord a similar low density of stone artifacts was found… without an animal carcass… No cut marks are visible on the bones… Gnawing, probably by a wolf…”

### Claim R4-008
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble treats four major preservation environments for archaeological locales in this period: **fluvial, lacustrine, loess, and caves**; all nine European regions were occupied.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`, `locale`  
**Grounding:** “The excavation of cave and open locales shows that all nine regions (Figure 3.1) were occupied during this period. Four major environments for the preservation of archaeological locales need to be considered: fluvial, lacustrine, loess and caves.”

### Claim R4-009
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble reports **first settlement of the northeast sector** (western Russia, Moldavia, Ukraine river valleys: Ketrosy, Korman IV, Molodova I & V, Khotylevo I, Il’skaya) in this period; last-interglacial sites on the Dnestr are unknown; many occupations are Early Glacial/Pleniglacial.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `neanderthals`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Notes:** NEW SITES — Molodova, Khotylevo I, Il’skaya, Korman IV, Ketrosy.  
**Grounding:** “the evidence from the northeast sector… was the first time that it was settled… Sites of last interglacial age are unknown (Hoffecker 1987:270). Instead, Middle Palaeolithic occupation on the Dnestr terraces…”

### Claim R4-010
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble notes a great rise in caves/abris preserving archaeology in this period, concentrated in limestone regions; classic SW France flagship sites include **Grotte Vaufrey**, La Micoque, Pech de l’Azé II, Combe Grenal, La Chaise, La Quina; plus sink-holes Coudoulous (98% bison) and La Borde (93% aurochs).  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `mousterian-industry`, `la-cotte-de-st-brelade`  
**Notes:** NEW SITES — Grotte Vaufrey, Combe Grenal, La Quina, Coudoulous, La Borde, Pech de l’Azé; also El Castillo (Cantabria).  
**Grounding:** “this period generally sees a great rise in the number of caves and abris… The classic area… is southwest France. The flagship sites are represented by the Grotte Vaufrey… Also in the southwest region is El Castillo cave…”

### Claim R4-011
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble argues northern vs southern cave excavation scales track artifact density: northern caves often emptied for few artifacts; southern excavators often stopped after ~100 retouched tools for typology — implying predictable continental variation that may reflect **differing long-term use intensity**.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `gamble-clive`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Grounding:** “in the north whole caves were regularly emptied… Total, or near-total clearance is less common in the southern… the archaeological evidence from caves varies in predictable ways across the continent and may reflect differing intensities in the long-term use of the northern and of the southern/Mediterranean regions.”

### Claim R4-012
**Register:** attributed (theoretical framing)  
**Claim:** Gamble’s Ch.5 approach uses three **palaeolithic rhythms** (paths/tracks, chaîne opératoire/gestures, taskscape) to ask what environments Neanderthals could cope with, treating technical acts as social acts.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `palaeolithic-rhythms`, `gamble-clive`, `neanderthals`  
**Grounding:** “The approach… is to examine the three rhythms which link the varied scales of analysis… These rhythms will concentrate on the first question… about Neanderthal societies — what environments could they cope with?… technical acts are also social acts…”

### Claim R4-013
**Register:** attributed (finding via Geneste)  
**Claim:** At **Grotte Vaufrey**, raw materials remain **predominantly local**: sources 1–2 dominate (~48% and 42% in richest level VIII); distant sources 5–9 (up to 80 km) never individually exceed 2% and are invariably retouched pieces.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `chaine-operatoire`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Notes:** NEW SITE — Grotte Vaufrey.  
**Grounding:** “At Grotte Vaufrey nine different flint sources… The raw materials are dominated by sources 1 and 2, 48 and 42 per cent respectively… The distant sources 5-9… up to 80km from the cave, never individually account for more than 2 per cent… invariably retouched pieces (Geneste 1988b).”

### Claim R4-014
**Register:** attributed (finding via Callow)  
**Claim:** At **La Cotte** (Jersey), no flint is on the island; nearest outcrop now submerged ~20 km north (25 m sea-level fall needed); Callow argues sources used were always close, probably **within ~3 km** (beach pebbles), with knapping flake:retouched ratios matching Geneste’s local-use expectations (<10 km).  
**Targets:** `la-cotte-de-st-brelade`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “There is no flint on the island and the nearest outcrop is now submerged c. 20 km north… As sea levels changed so different flint sources seem to have been used but… these were always close to the cave, probably within 3km… The overwhelming proportions… lead us to expect that sources within 10km were predominantly utilized.”

### Claim R4-015
**Register:** attributed (synthesis of Féblot-Augustins)  
**Claim:** In Féblot-Augustins’s inventory of **578 raw-material transfers** (OIS 7–4), very few exceed 80 km and most are under Geneste’s **20 km** threshold: Lower Palaeolithic ~75% <20 km; Early Middle Palaeolithic ~65% <20 km; Late Middle Palaeolithic Western Europe ~83% <20 km, Central Europe only ~48% <20 km with **19% >80 km**.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `middle-paleolithic`, `neanderthals`  
**Grounding:** Table 5.7 and text: “In her inventory of 578 raw-material transfers in the period OIS 7 to 4, Féblot-Augustins… found that very few exceeded 80 km and most were less than Geneste’s threshold distance of 20km (Table 5.7).”

### Claim R4-016
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble reports the longest cited transfers include Kulna level 11 Jurassic flint from Kraków (**230 km**, 0.7% of assemblage, introduced as blanks/rough cores — contra Geneste’s distant=retouched principle) and Schweinskopf **120 km** (yet 96% of stone from <4 km).  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Notes:** NEW SITES — Kulna, Schweinskopf.  
**Grounding:** “One of the largest distances… Kulna… 12 pieces out of 1,713… Jurassic flints from Krakow… 230km… introduced… as blanks and rough cores… Schweinskopf… largest distance… 120 km… 96 per cent… from less than 4km away.”

### Claim R4-017
**Register:** attributed (interpretive)  
**Claim:** Gamble interprets **longer raw-material transfers in north-central vs southwest** Europe as larger foraging tracks under increased continentality and resource separation in the plaid mosaic (also Roebroeks et al. 1988; Floss 1994).  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `gamble-clive`, `social-landscape-palaeolithic`, `neanderthals`  
**Grounding:** “The data show that raw-material transfers are considerably longer in the north-central than in the southwest region… These greater distances… can be interpreted as larger foraging tracks resulting from increased continentality and the separation of resources within the plaid mosaic…”

### Claim R4-018
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble notes environmental pressure on range size within SW France: Wiirm I (Early Glacial) vs Wiirm II (mostly Pleniglacial) shows **increased absolute and average transfer distances** with colder open conditions and red deer→reindeer faunal shifts.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `neanderthals`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Grounding:** “The effect of environmental pressure on hominid range size can also be seen between the Wiirm I… and Wiirm II… The increase in both the absolute and average distances… coincides with the shift towards more open, colder climatic conditions… red deer to reindeer…”

### Claim R4-019
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Apart from small local ochre at **Maastricht-Belvédére**, Gamble finds **no evidence for non-stone raw-material transfers** in this period.  
**Targets:** `maastricht-belvedere`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`  
**Grounding:** “Apart from small amounts of ochre found at Maastricht-Belvédére… there is no evidence for the transfer of raw materials other than stone. The hematite is thought to have been picked up locally in the Maas valley.”

### Claim R4-020
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble reports workshop/quarry locales on coarse local materials (Reutersruh quartzite, Markkleeberg flint, northern French workshops, Khotylevo I, Canjuers) hard to date but believed Saalian to end-Pleniglacial; Markkleeberg placed in OIS 8.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`, `acheulean-industry`  
**Grounding:** “workshop/quarries where coarser-grained local raw materials were worked at source… All of these quarry/workshop sites are difficult to date but are believed to be of Saalian to end-of-Pleniglacial age. Markkleeberg is placed in OIS 8 (Mania 1995).”

### Claim R4-021
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble stresses important lithic developments: preparation/predetermination of flakes and blades (**Levallois** and blade core reduction); soft-hammer prismatic blades dated to at least **~100 Kyr BP**, once thought exclusively Upper Palaeolithic post-40 Kyr — stressing unity of early Palaeolithic technology toward elaboration of **débitage** rather than façonnage.  
**Targets:** `levallois`, `chaine-operatoire`, `middle-paleolithic`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “What is surprising… is the dating of soft hammer, prismatic blades to at least 100 Kyr BP when such techniques were once regarded as exclusively Upper Palaeolithic… the major trend is towards the elaboration of systems of débitage rather than fagonnage…”

### Claim R4-022
**Register:** attributed (historiography of debate)  
**Claim:** Gamble presents the **Bordes/Binford Mousterian variability debate** as extended to settlement systems, mobility, raw material, chaîne opératoire, cultural transmission/cognition (Mithen), and chronology; Bordes’ five-tribe model is elaborated and, for Quina, Turq concludes it is **not a culture** but time-economizing + local good raw material + specialized activities; Bordes’ *évolution buisonnante* still applies.  
**Targets:** `binford-bordes-mousterian-debate`, `bordes-francois`, `binford-lewis`, `mithen-steven`, `mousterian-industry`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “The original Bordes/Binford debate… is authoritatively presented… by Mellars (1996)… Bordes’ view that five Neanderthal tribes made five different lithic assemblages… Turq concludes… Quina Mousterian… is not a culture, in Bordes’ sense… combination of time-economizing procedures, the availability of good, local raw material and… specialized… activities… Variability is also explained by… cognition (Mithen 1993b, 1996)… évolution buisonnante…”

### Claim R4-023
**Register:** attributed (method description)  
**Claim:** Gamble summarizes **Bordian analysis**: 63 flake types +  scrapers/points critical; real vs essential counts; cumulative percentage curves yield five repeated assemblage profiles; technical indices include IL (Levallois), ILam (blades), IR (side scrapers).  
**Targets:** `bordes-francois`, `mousterian-industry`, `methods` (descriptive), `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Among the flakes some sixty-three types are recognized… Two counts… real count… essential count… Five repeated profiles… technical indices… incidence of levallois working (IL)… blades (ILam)… side scrapers (IR).”

### Claim R4-024
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Bifaces continue throughout the period; **Micoquian** links northern-province assemblages (Belgium–Germany–Poland–Czech/Slovak–western Russia, Crimea, Caucasus); many bifaces are knives (Bocksteinmesser, pradniks with tranchet resharpening).  
**Targets:** `acheulean-industry`, `mousterian-industry`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “The Bockstein assemblage is regarded as a variant within the Micoquian tradition that links assemblages throughout the northern province… bifaces… aptly described as knives… Pradnik bifaces are widespread in the European Middle Palaeolithic.”

### Claim R4-025
**Register:** attributed (via Boëda on Biache)  
**Claim:** Gamble presents Boëda’s **recurrent Levallois** at Biache-St-Vaast level IIA (late OIS 7/early OIS 6) as chaîne opératoire dynamic (gesture/tradition), not Bordes’ static finished-form classification; knappers master **actions of making**, not merely pre-determined shapes — Levallois as bodily discipline of gesture sequences.  
**Targets:** `levallois`, `chaine-operatoire`, `agency`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Notes:** NEW SITE — Biache-St-Vaast.  
**Grounding:** “Boéda’s aim is to understand lithic collections on the basis of the dynamic of technology… rather than… the static approach of Bordes… Rather than learning the artifact’s shape… the knappers master the actions of making… a technique which disciplines the body to perform a routine sequence of gestures…”

### Claim R4-026
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble argues Levallois is **not solely raw-material-determined**: used on quartzites and ‘inferior’ materials (Reutersruh, Pontnewydd volcanic rocks) as well as high-quality flint (Bakers Hole).  
**Targets:** `levallois`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “the levallois technique also used quartzites and other materials which we regard as inferior to flint. The workshop/quarry of Reutersruh… and the volcanic rocks used at Pontnewydd cave… another.”

### Claim R4-027
**Register:** attributed (via Geneste at Vaufrey)  
**Claim:** Geneste’s chaîne opératoire at Grotte Vaufrey has **27 technological categories** in four phases (0 selection → 1 initial shaping → 2 débitage → 3 retouch); level VIII shows complete chaînes confirmed by refitting; shifts analysis from Bordes typology toward sequence + regional mobility, enabling individual action via refits/transfers.  
**Targets:** `chaine-operatoire`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Grounding:** “There are twenty-seven technological categories in his chaine opératoire… four phases… The presence of complete chaines opératoires… confirmed by refitting… typological/technological comparisons… now operate at a more dynamic level… related… to individual action.”

### Claim R4-028
**Register:** attributed (via Turq)  
**Claim:** **Quina Mousterian** (mostly Pleniglacial OIS 4) is scraper-dominated with IL <10%; flexible non-Levallois débitage producing supports; mostly caves/rockshelters; **provisioning territories 5–10 km**; Quina artifacts **not landscape-transported** unlike Levallois assemblages; contrasts with MTA (OIS 3) territories 15–20 km.  
**Targets:** `mousterian-industry`, `levallois`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `binford-bordes-mousterian-debate`  
**Grounding:** “Quina variant… mostly dates to the Pleniglacial OIS 4… levallois index of less than 10 per cent… very few examples… outside cave and rock shelters. The provisioning territories are very small, between 5 and 10km… Quina artifacts are not transported around the landscape, unlike those made… with the levallois technique…”

### Claim R4-029
**Register:** attributed (critique)  
**Claim:** Gamble argues Turq’s local Quina raw materials **contradict Rolland & Dibble’s model** that Quina = distant materials / intensive retouch of imports; their local:distant categories are qualitative and model-fitting rather than scaled tests — once distances are added, explanatory power declines.  
**Targets:** `binford-bordes-mousterian-debate`, `mousterian-industry`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `gamble-clive`  
**Grounding:** “Turq’s findings are therefore at variance with the predictions from Rolland and Dibble’s model… Quina assemblages will be made on distant raw materials… This contradiction identifies a problem… they have qualitatively determined these two distance categories… As Turq shows… Quina assemblages are made on local rather than distant flint.”

### Claim R4-030
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Gamble dates **widespread Levallois** to the Saalian after ~300 Kyr BP (Bridgland: OIS 8 on Lower Thames; rare same-age Somme; conceptual link to biface façonnage at Cagny la Garenne OIS 11–12). White & Pettitt: Levallois appearance is the only major technological innovation 500–200 Kyr BP justifying Lower/Middle Palaeolithic division.  
**Targets:** `levallois`, `middle-paleolithic`, `acheulean-industry`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Bridgland… proposes that the technique first appears widely in OIS 8… it is possible that the widespread use of levallois… may first appear in the Saalian complex, after 300 Kyr BP… White and Pettitt (1995:33) are correct in identifying the appearance of levallois as the only major technological innovation in the period 500-200 Kyr BP…”

### Claim R4-031
**Register:** attributed (via Geneste; interpretive)  
**Claim:** Geneste shows the **most mobile Mousterian elements** (distant raw materials) are Levallois products — transport of Levallois flakes promotes flexibility under less predictable environments; bifaces less flexible. Gamble prefers mobility-tactics reading (Kuhn 1995) over linguistic/pre-planning readings of Levallois.  
**Targets:** `levallois`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `agency`, `neanderthals`  
**Grounding:** “Geneste’s (1989:83) study… the most mobile elements of any Mousterian technology… involve levallois elements. The transportation of levallois flakes assisted hominids in adapting to less predictable environmental conditions by promoting flexibility… more fruitful to examine the technique in terms of the tactics of individual mobility (Kuhn 1995).”

### Claim R4-032
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Northern **Middle Palaeolithic Blade Group** (Early Glacial into Pleniglacial: Crayford, Seclin, Wallertheim, Tönchesberg, Riencourt-lès-Bapaume) shows dual reduction (Levallois + non-Levallois Upper Palaeolithic-like prismatic blades) but **very few blanks retouched to standard types** — “an Upper Palaeolithic technology that lacks Upper Palaeolithic types.”  
**Targets:** `middle-paleolithic`, `levallois`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “what makes this blade production very different from the Upper Palaeolithic are the very small numbers of blanks which are then retouched to standard types. It is, if you like, an Upper Palaeolithic technology that lacks Upper Palaeolithic types.”

### Claim R4-033
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Organic technology remains limited: Salzgitter-Lebenstedt reindeer-antler pick; Lehringen yew spear and older Schöningen spears as short chaînes/façonnage; most ‘symbolic’ engraved/pierced pieces argued natural by D’Errico & Villa (1997); Tata polished mammoth lamellar noted as unique/enigmatic.  
**Targets:** `schoningen`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `behavioral-modernity`, `neanderthals`  
**Notes:** NEW SITE — Lehringen; Tata.  
**Grounding:** “at Salzgitter-Lebenstedt an unshed reindeer antler has been chopped… The Lehringen yew spear… Schöningen spears… most of the oft-cited engraved and pierced pieces are entirely natural (D’Errico and Villa 1997)… polished mammoth lamellar from Tata…”

### Claim R4-034
**Register:** attributed (via Kuhn/Binford)  
**Claim:** Gamble uses Binford forager/collector model + Kuhn: evidence of reuse/resharpening does **not** measure curation intensity alone but mobility/land-use pattern; Quina high retouch on local high-grade stone undermines Dibble’s import-conservation resharpening story for transverse scrapers.  
**Targets:** `binford-lewis`, `mousterian-industry`, `ethnoarchaeology`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “evidence for reuse… does not provide a measure of the extent to which artifacts were maintained or curated, but instead reflects the pattern of mobility and land use… For Dibble… transverse Quina scraper is the final chapter… expectation… conserved valuable imported raw materials, but… this variant is usually made on local stone…”

### Claim R4-035
**Register:** attributed (via Cziesla)  
**Claim:** Three refit types: production sequences (*Aufeinanderpassungen* — common at Maastricht-Belvédére, Vaufrey, Biache); broken artifacts (*Aneinanderpassungen* — no Middle Palaeolithic between-locale distant refits); modifications (*Anpassungen* — e.g. La Cotte long sharpening flakes n=2,195, only one refitted).  
**Targets:** `maastricht-belvedere`, `la-cotte-de-st-brelade`, `chaine-operatoire`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “three kinds of refit… Refitting of production sequences… most common… Maastricht-Belvédére, Grotte Vaufrey and Biache St. Vaast… no examples of refitting broken artifacts between distant locales… numerous (n=2,195) specialized long sharpening flakes at La Cotte… only one could be refitted…”

### Claim R4-036
**Register:** attributed (via Roebroeks et al.)  
**Claim:** High-density lithic locales with abundant refits may reflect **tool maintenance** (core prep, blank production, retouch); artifact-poor locales with few refits may mark specific activities — possible spatial differentiation vs earlier Boxgrove where all three refit patterns occur.  
**Targets:** `maastricht-belvedere`, `boxgrove`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “locales with a high density of lithics and abundant refitting may reflect tool maintenance activities… Artifactually poor locales with few refittings may relate to specific activities… This may mark a differentiation of space from earlier locales such as Boxgrove…”

### Claim R4-037
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** First movement onto Russian/Ukrainian plains; NE region settled late, evidence points to first colonization in **Eemian** rather than Saalian (Hoffecker 1987).  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Grounding:** “It is during this period that the first movement onto the plains of Russia and the Ukraine took place. The NE region… first colonization during the Eemian rather than during the earlier Saalian complex (Hoffecker 1987).”

### Claim R4-038
**Register:** attributed (via Soffer; Gamble concurs pattern)  
**Claim:** Soffer argues Neanderthals preferred **greatest topographic variety** (dense plaid mosaic): continuous presence in broken Dnestr–Prut/Crimea/Caucasus vs sporadic Middle Desna plains; environmental degradation on plain proper more unpredictable than diversified foothill/canyon settings.  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `social-landscape-palaeolithic`  
**Grounding:** “Soffer… preferred conditions for Neanderthal people… areas of greatest topographic variety… more continuous human presence in some parts… more sporadic discontinuous presence… Middle Desna… resources on the plain proper far more unpredictable than in the Dnestr–Prut region and in the Crimea.”

### Claim R4-039
**Register:** attributed (Gamble’s habitat thesis)  
**Claim:** Gamble reasserts absence of hominids in England during Eemian optimum (OIS 5e) despite *Hippopotamus* to Yorkshire as evidence they preferred **not mature oceanic deciduous forest** (low prey density/predictability) — social organization, not mere presence of trees, limited intensification.  
**Targets:** `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `neanderthals`  
**Grounding:** “the absence from England of hominids during the climatic optimum of the Eemian (OIS 5e) is a pointer to the structure of the environments they preferred. The mature deciduous forests… reduced the predictability and biomass… dietary requirements could not be met without some form of intensification of effort.”

### Claim R4-040
**Register:** attributed (position recording)  
**Claim:** Gamble critiques Roebroeks, Auguste & Conard for concluding Saalian hominids could live ‘anywhere’ from European interglacial sites (Weimar travertines, Lehringen elephant); he counters with January 0°C isotherm / oceanicity–continentality effects on mosaic structure and winter resources — no food-storage pits known; data currently favor limited social factors on transferable skills.  
**Targets:** `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `neanderthals`, `maastricht-belvedere`  
**Grounding:** “They ignore the global picture… Blessed with such powers, their ‘decision’ to remain in a restricted part of the old world… I would suggest… Saalian hominids had skills which could be readily transferred… but that these were still limited by social factors as expressed through gesture and material transfers on a routine and often immediate basis.”

### Claim R4-041
**Register:** attributed (via Gamble 1995e data)  
**Claim:** In NC-region caves, Middle Palaeolithic faunas average **~5 herbivore and ~3.4 large carnivore species** vs palaeontological (non-lithic) faunas ~2.72 herbivores / 2.5 carnivores — hominids compete with major carnivores in highest-prey-biomass, tightly focused mosaic patches.  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Grounding:** “In these 126 assemblages there are on average 5 herbivore and 3.4 large carnivore species… palaeontological… herbivores declines to 2.72… carnivores… 2.5… Within their taskscapes the hominids are competing with the major carnivores in areas of the highest prey biomass.”

### Claim R4-042
**Register:** attributed (via Stiner / Gaudzinski)  
**Claim:** Growing evidence Neanderthals concentrated on particular taxa **and regularly took prime-aged prey** (Wallertheim B1 bison, La Borde aurochs 93%, Il’skaya, Biache aurochs >90% prime, low carnivore gnawing) — evolution of hominid niche as intercept hunters of prime-aged prey (only lion among other large carnivores similarly takes primes).  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Notes:** NEW SITES — Wallertheim, La Borde, Biache, Il’skaya, Erd, Taubach.  
**Grounding:** “There is growing evidence that Neanderthals not only concentrated upon particular taxa… but that they also regularly took prime-aged prey… only lion among the other large carnivores takes prime condition animals… Hyena and wolf mostly kill the young and the old.”

### Claim R4-043
**Register:** attributed (interpretive conclusion)  
**Claim:** Gamble answers Q1 (environments): Neanderthals expanded habitat range via longer chaînes, farther transfers, alternative food-management — **prime-age hunters without external symbol systems** inscribed on landscape or objects at gatherings; being social was embodied in gestures/negotiations of the taskscape.  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `behavioral-modernity`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Neanderthals appear to have become prime-age hunters without developing an external system of symbols that either were inscribed on the landscape… or involved objects at gatherings… the transferable skill was still being social… based on engagement with, rather than analysis of, the world…”

### Claim R4-044
**Register:** attributed (network model application)  
**Claim:** Skill transfer enabling long-term continental occupation worked by individuals **negotiating effective networks more intensively** via emotion, materials, and gesture-embedded temporal rhythms in mutual taskscapes.  
**Targets:** `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `gamble-clive`, `neanderthals`, `palaeolithic-rhythms`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “The transfer of skills… was achieved by individuals negotiating their effective networks more intensively. It stemmed from their use of the appropriate resources of emotion and materials, as expressed through gesture embedded in temporal rhythms.”

### Claim R4-045
**Register:** attributed (via Féblot-Augustins)  
**Claim:** Transfer patterns are **star-shaped in SW** and **predominantly linear in NC** (especially NE Hungary ~100 km); distances >100 km often traverse Carpathian watersheds and mostly fall 60–40 Kyr (Ch.6); below threshold always within circumscribed geographical units.  
**Targets:** `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `social-landscape-palaeolithic`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Féblot-Augustins (1993:239) has described the pattern of transfers as star-shaped in the SW region and predominantly linear in her sample from the NC region… few examples of distances greater than 100 km traverse the Carpathian watersheds… most of these large distances fall in the period 60-40 Kyr…”

### Claim R4-046
**Register:** attributed (via Geneste)  
**Claim:** Geneste reconstructs Grotte Vaufrey level VIII procurement territory ~**3,000 km²**; lithics testify to **fleeting occupations by small groups** for which raw-material supply was not major concern given mastery of débitage and ease of abandoning products/equipment.  
**Targets:** `locale`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `neanderthals`, `chaine-operatoire`  
**Grounding:** “Based on data from the Grotte Vaufrey level VIII he reconstructs a procurement territory around the cave of 3,000 km²… Les vestiges lithiques de la couche VIII peuvent témoigner d’occupations fugaces… par de petits groupes humains… l’approvisionnement de matières premières ne semblait pas être le souci majeur…”

### Claim R4-047
**Register:** attributed (via Kuhn; Gamble agrees)  
**Claim:** Kuhn’s distinction: **provisioning people** vs **provisioning places** with implements/raw materials; Neanderthals regularly carried tools as hedge against unforeseen needs; evidence for complex long-term provisioning of occupations with cores/raw materials is **elusive**.  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Grounding:** “Kuhn makes the useful heuristic distinction between provisioning people and provisioning places… ‘Neanderthals… regularly carried tools as a hedge against unforeseen, generalized needs. Evidence for more complex, long-term strategies such as the provisioning of occupations with cores or raw materials is elusive.’”

### Claim R4-048
**Register:** attributed (Geneste watershed; Gamble nuance)  
**Claim:** Geneste identifies Eemian as watershed: before, local materials dominate and few implements carried; after last interglacial, some spatial extension and larger subsistence territories with greater anticipation — but dimensions of Neanderthal **landscape of habit** and chaînes remain small.  
**Targets:** `social-landscape-palaeolithic`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Geneste (1990) identifies the Eemian as an important watershed… After the last interglacial there is some spatial extension… larger subsistence territories… But even so, the dimensions of the Neanderthal landscape of habit… are small.”

### Claim R4-049
**Register:** attributed (via Féblot-Augustins modes)  
**Claim:** Of eight lithic exploitation modes, **only Mode 8** (blanks/tools with no primary waste/nodules — often Levallois) occurs **>11 km from source**; distance systematically truncates chaînes opératoires.  
**Targets:** `levallois`, `chaine-operatoire`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “only the products of mode 8, blanks and tools with no primary waste or nodules, are found above 11km from source. Many of these mode 8 elements will be levallois products.”

### Claim R4-050
**Register:** attributed (core network conclusion)  
**Claim:** Within regional landscape of habit measured by transfers, **effective networks** structured social extension: life was **still very local and usually immediate**; dimensions of landscape of habit rarely exceeded; no non-stone materials transferred any distance.  
**Targets:** `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `social-landscape-palaeolithic`, `gamble-clive`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “I therefore conclude that life was still very local and usually immediate… the dimensions of the landscape of habit… were rarely exceeded… indicated by the lack of materials, other than stone, which were transferred over any distance.”

### Claim R4-051
**Register:** attributed (core network conclusion)  
**Claim:** No more elaborate **social landscape** than earlier periods: few longer transfers but no extension beyond routinization via symbols/inscription of place; **extended networks remained largely unnegotiated and undeveloped**; wider social ramifications carried by effective-network resources — so Neanderthal societies **did vary** across Europe (Q2 yes).  
**Targets:** `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `social-landscape-palaeolithic`, `behavioral-modernity`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Nor can I see additional evidence to argue for a more elaborate social landscape… Their extended networks remained largely unnegotiated and undeveloped… our second question — did Neanderthal societies vary across Europe? — must be answered positively…”

### Claim R4-052
**Register:** attributed (site interpretation)  
**Claim:** At **La Cotte** bone piles (layers 3 and 6): mammoth and woolly rhino remains; Scott argues headland used for two stampede/drive gatherings of prime mammoths + few rhinos over cliffs, then selected bones dragged under overhang; few stones among piles.  
**Targets:** `la-cotte-de-st-brelade`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `neanderthals`  
**Grounding:** “Scott (1986a:183) makes the case that the headland at La Cotte… was used for two separate gatherings when a small herd of prime mammoths accompanied by a few rhinos was driven over the cliff. Selected bones were then dragged up beneath the overhang.”

### Claim R4-053
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Lehringen and Gröbern (OIS 5e lakeside): ~25 and ~27 stone tools with elephant skeletons; Gröbern plan shows ≥5 raw materials with partly exclusive distributions around carcass — brief gatherings where **individuals identifiable by raw materials**; Lehringen spear association possibly fortuitous (wood floats).  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `locale`, `agency`  
**Notes:** NEW SITES — Lehringen, Gröbern (Grébern).  
**Grounding:** “Much easier to explain are the brief gatherings as at the Grébern elephant locale… where individuals can be identified by the different raw materials they used as they clustered around the carcass… At Lehringen… it is conceivable that the water brought two unrelated items together.”

### Claim R4-054
**Register:** attributed (via Stiner; Gamble comparison)  
**Claim:** Latium MP caves show exclusive carnivore / mixed / exclusive hominid levels; young-dominated carnivore age profiles mark denning; northern Hohlenstein-Stadel high carnivore % dominated by hibernating **bear** with prime (not U-shaped) hyena/wolf profiles — different regional skills: Italy optimizes landscape access to prey density; Germany competes for **prime-aged cohort** access.  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `middle-paleolithic`  
**Grounding:** “hominids in these two regions are employing some specific skills. In Italy, selection was for locales… with optimum access to prey density. In Germany competition for the use of locales was based on access to the most productive cohort (prime-aged animals)…”

### Claim R4-055
**Register:** attributed (Maastricht-Belvédére case study)  
**Claim:** **Maastricht-Belvédére** (Netherlands, OIS 7 unit IV): seven major excavated areas; local environment wet protected river margins with marsh, alder/ash, open deciduous woodland (molluscs); Site K richest (~10,912 lithics, 29.5/m², 137 retouched); Site N ‘background’ 0.6/m² over 765 m² — Roebroeks et al. ‘**veil of stones**’.  
**Targets:** `maastricht-belvedere`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `locale`  
**Grounding:** “Seven major areas… OIS 7 landscape… Site K… almost 11,000 lithic artifacts at a density of 29.5… Site N… ‘background noise’… 0.6 specimens per m²… Roebroeks et al. (1992) have described these low-density scatters… as a ‘veil of stones’.”

### Claim R4-056
**Register:** attributed (via Roebroeks)  
**Claim:** At Belvédére, rich sites may record **technology maintenance** (flakes/tools production) and poor sites **technology use** in tasks; few retouched pieces overall (Geneste stage 3); Levallois single and recurrent débitage common but scrapers rare; densities of retouched pieces far lower than Biache IIA (2.28/m² vs max Belvédére 0.37/m² at K).  
**Targets:** `maastricht-belvedere`, `levallois`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Notes:** Biache comparison.  
**Grounding:** “rich sites may be recording activities essentially aiming to maintain the technology… while the poor sites are where the technology was used… What unites all the Belvédére sites… is the very low number of retouched pieces… By comparison with Biache St. Vaast… density of retouched pieces is 2.28 per m², whereas the highest… at Belvédére is 0.37…”

### Claim R4-057
**Register:** attributed (Site C refitting)  
**Claim:** Belvédére Site C: 3,067 artifacts, six raw-material units, **22% refit by number / 70% by weight**; mostly production-sequence refits; pieces carried away or partially flaked material introduced; Marjorie’s core (RMU4) analyzed by Schlanger (1996); Stapert ring-and-sector: slight open-air hearth indication, **no hut signatures**.  
**Targets:** `maastricht-belvedere`, `chaine-operatoire`, `locale`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Twenty-two per cent of all the flint artifacts could be fitted back together, which accounts for 70 per cent by weight… RMU4, also known as Majorie’s core… Stapert… slight indications… open-air hearth. No hut signatures were found.”

### Claim R4-058
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Grotte Vaufrey level VIII: chipped stone density **85/m²** (high vs most open locales), retouched 1.6/m²; major lithic refits production sequences (max ~5 m vs Belvédére RMU2 up to 13 m); no proven hearths though charcoal concentration noted; Binford taphonomy finds cut marks absent, gnaw marks common.  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `binford-lewis`, `locale`  
**Grounding:** “All chipped stone occurs at a density of 85 per m²… retouched artifacts… 1.6 per m²… largest distance being a 5-m refit… at Grotte Vaufrey cut marks are absent while gnaw marks are common (Binford 1988).”

### Claim R4-059
**Register:** attributed (fireplaces / language)  
**Claim:** Best hearths in caves (Pech de l’Azé II scoops; Hauteroche); open sites generally lack convincing fireplaces (Belvédére charcoal/stones not artificial; La Cotte burning without hearths). Only **Grotte Lazaret** approaches hearth-seated horseshoe/size-sorted pattern around two wall charcoal concentrations (~3 m toss zone).  
**Targets:** `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `locale`, `behavioral-modernity`  
**Notes:** NEW SITE — Lazaret (Grotte Lazaret).  
**Grounding:** “What, with one possible exception, is currently lacking… is spatial evidence corresponding to the hearth seated model… The exception is the Grotte Lazaret… larger blocks lying 3m from the centre of the two hearths…”

### Claim R4-060
**Register:** attributed (language / network conclusion)  
**Claim:** Absence of hearth concentric patterns, built structures, pits, postholes leads Gamble to conclude **social occasions and places lacking**; gatherings depended neither on language nor personification of objects/locations; temporality set by immediate taskscape routines and effective-scale landscapes of habit; **extended networks weakly if at all developed** (Q3: social interaction not necessarily dependent on spoken language).  
**Targets:** `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `behavioral-modernity`, `locale`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “I conclude that social occasions and places… were also lacking… depended neither on language nor on the personification of objects and locations… Their extended networks were only weakly, if at all, developed… while it is good to think that Neanderthals had an advanced form of communication, it was only directed towards the negotiation of their intimate and effective networks.”

### Claim R4-061
**Register:** attributed (vs Aiello & Dunbar)  
**Claim:** Gamble engages Aiello & Dunbar (neocortex→group size ~150 by 250 Kyr; gossip language): longer transfers, focused hunting, Levallois chaînes show new temporality, but spatial record not dramatically different from Boxgrove/Bilzingsleben or across nine regions — selection for vocal communication from **intensity and limited spatial extension of interaction**, not group size; would not select for full symbolic language using material culture as external referent.  
**Targets:** `behavioral-modernity`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `boxgrove`, `bilzingsleben`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “what we do not see from the spatial evidence is any dramatic change in the record from that seen much earlier at Boxgrove or Bilzingsleben… The selection pressure for such vocal communication came not from group size but from the intensity and limited spatial extension of social interaction.”

### Claim R4-062
**Register:** attributed  
**Claim:** Little evidence for co-operation requiring symbolic language to stretch relations across time/space: Biache/Tönchesberg/Erd simple zonation of materials; Vaufrey areas for bone cracking/knapping/fire but Binford stresses carnivore use; co-operation was **routine negotiation within intimate and effective networks**, not an external organizing principle.  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `agency`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Evidence is lacking for co-operation in the form of architecture and the structured use of space… Co-operation was therefore a routine act, a negotiated outcome between hominids exploring their intimate and effective networks. It was not an external principle…”

### Claim R4-063
**Register:** attributed (Belvédére taskscape vignette)  
**Claim:** Gamble’s social reading of Maastricht-Belvédére: short-distance material carry; knapping often solitary yet effective-network-linked via regional traditions; co-presence respects personal space (longest refits ~10 m = face-to-face); ‘veil of stones’ as metaphor for continuous regional but punctuated local social life; dwelling/taskscape not ecosystem adaptation; social integration not system integration.  
**Targets:** `maastricht-belvedere`, `agency`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “I strongly suspect that several of the knapping episodes were solitary performances… Personal space was respected… performance space remained small, no more than the rare but longest refit of 10m… What happened… was not system integration… Individuals did not cease to have a social life because they were by themselves… They dwelt in the world at Maastricht-Belvédére, they did not adapt to it…”

### Claim R4-064
**Register:** attributed (Chapter 5 summary — three answers)  
**Claim:** Summary answers: (1) Yes, greater habitat range but **not all habitats**; (2) Yes, social life **varied** across continent; (3) No, interaction **not necessarily dependent on spoken language** but involved communication dedicated to social relations. Society founded on **intimate and effective networks** individuals routinely constructed.  
**Targets:** `neanderthals`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `behavioral-modernity`, `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Neanderthal society was founded on the intimate and effective networks… Yes, they could cope with a greater range, but not with all types of habitat; Yes… Neanderthal social life varied across the continent; No, such social interaction was not necessarily dependent on spoken language but did involve a system of communication dedicated to social relations.”

### Claim R4-065
**Register:** attributed (summary technology–society)  
**Claim:** New temporality: longer manufacturing sequences and larger raw-material catchments; contrast Arago vs Vaufrey circulation/chaînes; shift from biface façonnage dominance toward **débitage** (Levallois/prismatic blades); Levallois–distant raw material association tracks enhanced individual mobility; material culture patterning ascribed to strong intimate/effective networks and **absence of elaborate time–space distanciation via symbols**.  
**Targets:** `levallois`, `chaine-operatoire`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `behavioral-modernity`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “we can see a new temporality to action expressed through longer manufacturing sequences and the larger areas from which raw materials came… The association of the levallois technique with the use of more distant raw materials… These were societies built on co-presence… The evidence is for social rather than system integration…”

### Claim R4-066
**Register:** attributed (summary landscape of habit)  
**Claim:** Neanderthals lived in **landscapes of habit** not a social landscape: land-use dimensions vary by region with climate/resource distribution; life local; longer transfers still within ~**2–3 days’ walking** of locales; hematite scraps and Tata plaque do not indicate negotiated acts beyond co-presence using resources unavailable to intimate/effective networks.  
**Targets:** `social-landscape-palaeolithic`, `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “Neanderthals lived in landscapes of habit rather than a social landscape… temporal span of action around individual locales of about two to three days’ walking distance. Evidence for the social landscape is lacking. A few pieces of hematite, some enigmatic bone and ivory objects such as the Tata plaque, do not betoken…”

### Claim R4-067
**Register:** attributed (summary intensification)  
**Claim:** Neanderthals **intensified social life** by elaborating non-symbolic gesture and material resources; intimate network expanded toward quasi-effective scale (larger numbers, more co-operation at animal gatherings and workshops) using emotional resources of intimacy; language capacity used to enhance emotional resources of a ‘caring’ society rather than self-defining symbolic system (later development).  
**Targets:** `intimate-effective-extended-networks`, `behavioral-modernity`, `neanderthals`, `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “the Neanderthals intensified social life. They elaborated the non-symbolic use of gesture and material resources… their intimate network expanded and became a quasi-effective one… while Neanderthals had the capacity for language… all they used it for was as a means to enhance their use of the emotional resources… Call them the original caring society if you like…”

### Claim R4-068
**Register:** attributed (Ch.6 opening frame — transition)  
**Claim:** Gamble frames 60–21 Kyr BP as transition from Neanderthal to Cro-Magnon anatomy/culture/behaviour and Middle to Upper Palaeolithic; majority view modern human origins in **Africa** (anatomical/genetic); European replacement a **slow ~20,000-year** process with regionally specific prehistory; rejects institutional ‘Moderns arrival’ narratives that render Neanderthals powerless agents; transition as individuals becoming creative agents **beyond co-presence rules**.  
**Targets:** `modern-human-origins-debate`, `neanderthals`, `behavioral-modernity`, `stringer-chris`, `gamble-clive`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`  
**Grounding:** “a majority view has emerged that on the basis of anatomical and genetic evidence the origin of modern people was in Africa… replacement in this continent was a slow process, lasting some 20,000 years… the transition… was about the emergence of individuals as creative agents beyond the limitations set by the rules of co-presence.”

### Claim R4-069
**Register:** attributed (Ch.6 dating hygiene)  
**Claim:** Interpleniglacial ~58–28 Kyr BP = OIS 3 into early OIS 2; LGM downturn 28–21; ice maximum 20–18. AMS C14 can date to ~45 Kyr but bulk samples risk contamination younging (1% modern C can turn 67→37 Kyr); radiocarbon uncalibrated in this range (Gamble uses lowercase **bp**); Abric Romani U-series vs AMS suggests ~**5.6±1.5 Ka** offset — Spanish Aurignacian may be as old as ~43 Kyr BP.  
**Targets:** `radiocarbon-dating`, `thermoluminescence-dating`, `gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999`, `modern-human-origins-debate`  
**Grounding:** “a sample with a true age of 67 Kyr will give a date of 37 Kyr on the introduction of only 1 per cent present-day carbon… Bischoff estimates… difference between radiocarbon and U-series is a startling 5.6+1.5 Ka… the Aurignacian… may be as old as 43 Kyr BP… I use the lower case bp to indicate uncalibrated ages.”

## Coverage actually read
- Slice lines 1–~946: mostly garbled OCR tables (absolute dates Table 5.1–5.2, correlation charts) — partial recovery only via later prose referencing tables.
- Readable continuous prose ~lines 947–4476 (Ch.5 remainder through Summary).
- Ch.6 opening ~4477–end of slice (framework + dating; site tables garbled).

## Claim count
**69 claims** (R4-001 … R4-069).
